And the Smithsonian Institutes National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress. And today I will be the interviewer. The whole of this, the Pacific Ocean is behind this, well, well. It's a great honor. And I think that, you know, this was a key part of American history and world history. And there are so many lessons for the world to know about how you try and change things without violence. And so I'm more than happy to be a part of this. Thank you so much. So, Will, I wanted, if I could ask you to start just by telling us about where you grew up and how you grew up and how you may have seen some of the lessons that you learned early on playing out in the work that you chose. Born in New York in 46, but my dad had just gotten back from World War II. He had a very good chiropractic practice in Saginaw, Michigan when he went in the war. My mother, who was a teacher and later a college professor at USC, a special ed, went back and lived with her family in Staten Island. And when my dad got out, I had to sort of start over again, you know, after a couple years away for service. And, you know, that was when the migration was happening to California. And apparently he talked to some people in Michigan and they said, yeah, there's a lot of opportunity out there. My mother worked in the campaign of the first Hispanic councilman and then later congressmen. But I'm blanking on his name. The federal building is named for him downtown. I'll think of it later. Roybal, Ed Roybal. Since we become friends with somebody at Cal State LLA, did a book about Ed Roybal. My sister had talked to Ed Roybal before he died. His daughter, Lucille Roybal, Allard, has his seat. He remembered my mother. My mother was an activist. And my grandfather had been the assistant superintendent of New York City Schools, Morrissey Segal, and had handled all the veterans retreating and all the vocational education after World War II. So I come from a family of educators on my mom's side. My dad were also immigrants. His father and mother were immigrants as my grandparents on both sides. And they had shoe stores. They had shoe stores. And then the typical next generation became a college educated. My dad was the only one of his generation to be college educated. So we lived in the projects for a couple of years. And I think that shaped me in the sense I saw pictures in the book you have of Hispanic kids, Asian kids, black kids, all living in that.
